gold.rationale: The article accurately reflects the March 12, 2026 rumor-debunking notice and preserves its main findings: the screenshots were not evidence of a real loss event, the original creator said they were a joke, and the claimed WeChat red-packet workflow was not technically possible as described.

atomic_points.post_summary: Chinese authorities and the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform denied on March 12, 2026, that AI tools OpenClaw or QClaw could automatically send WeChat red packets, citing technical limitations and identifying the viral screenshots as a joke.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Chinese rumor-checking authorities said on March 12, 2026 that a viral online claim about OpenClaw or QClaw causing WeChat users to lose money was false.
atoms: Chinese rumor-checking authorities issued a statement on March 12, 2026.
The statement addressed a viral online claim about OpenClaw or QClaw.
The viral claim alleged that OpenClaw or QClaw caused WeChat users to lose money.
Chinese authorities declared the claim about OpenClaw or QClaw causing financial loss to be false.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The claim, which spread in posts and chat screenshots, said the tools could be connected to WeChat and instructed to send red packets automatically.
atoms: The claim spread via online posts and chat screenshots.
The claim stated that OpenClaw or QClaw could be connected to WeChat.
The claim stated that the tools could be instructed to send red packets automatically.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The denial was published by the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform, which cited Chengdu cyberspace rumor-refuting sources and an account called Rumor Filter.
atoms: The China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform published the denial.
The platform cited Chengdu cyberspace rumor-refuting sources.
The platform cited an account called Rumor Filter.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The posts that circulated online relied on screenshots from group chats.
atoms: Circulating online posts relied on screenshots from group chats.

sentence_level.sentence_text: They claimed OpenClaw or QClaw could be linked to WeChat and then told to send red packets, the in-app cash gifting feature widely used on the platform, without the user taking the usual action required inside the app.
atoms: The posts claimed OpenClaw or QClaw could be linked to WeChat.
The posts claimed the tools could be told to send red packets.
Red packets are described as an in-app cash gifting feature widely used on WeChat.
The claim suggested red packets could be sent without the user taking usual in-app actions.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In one version of the story, a user was said to have been “brushed,” or charged, 600 yuan after logging in and giving instructions.
atoms: One version of the story claimed a user was charged 600 yuan.
The term “brushed” is used to describe being charged.
The alleged charge occurred after the user logged in and gave instructions.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The way the screenshots were framed suggested that an AI assistant had triggered a payment through WeChat.
atoms: The framing of the screenshots suggested an AI assistant triggered a payment.
The suggested payment was made through WeChat.

sentence_level.sentence_text: According to the rumor-refuting platform, those images did not show a real case.
atoms: The rumor-refuting platform stated the images did not show a real case.

sentence_level.sentence_text: It said the screenshots were part of a joke or meme-style post made by an internet user, not evidence of an actual loss.
atoms: The platform stated the screenshots were part of a joke or meme-style post.
The post was made by an internet user.
The platform stated the screenshots were not evidence of an actual loss.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The original poster later said the images were not documenting a real incident and had been created in a playful, attention-grabbing style.
atoms: The original poster stated the images were not documenting a real incident.
The original poster stated the images were created in a playful, attention-grabbing style.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That later explanation cut directly against the main claim in the viral screenshots, which had been reposted and discussed as though they showed a genuine case of money being lost.
atoms: The original poster's explanation contradicted the main claim in the viral screenshots.
The viral screenshots were reposted and discussed as showing a genuine case of money loss.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The debunking notice also pointed to a technical problem with the story.
atoms: The debunking notice pointed to a technical problem with the story.

sentence_level.sentence_text: QClaw, it said, currently supports deployment on computers, not as a direct mobile-side tool operating inside the phone version of WeChat.
atoms: The notice stated QClaw currently supports deployment on computers.
The notice stated QClaw is not a direct mobile-side tool operating inside the phone version of WeChat.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That matters because the online claim described a simple process in which the assistant could be given a command and then carry out a red-packet payment inside WeChat just as shown in the screenshots.
atoms: The online claim described a process where an assistant carries out red-packet payments inside WeChat.
The claim suggested this process matched what was shown in the screenshots.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The notice said that setup was not technically possible in the form presented because WeChat’s desktop version does not support sending red packets.
atoms: The notice stated the setup was not technically possible in the form presented.
The reason given was that WeChat’s desktop version does not support sending red packets.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Public reporting on the PC version of WeChat has described some limited red-packet-related functions on desktop, but not desktop-based sending in the way the viral posts described.
atoms: Public reporting describes limited red-packet-related functions on the PC version of WeChat.
Public reporting states the PC version does not support desktop-based sending as described in viral posts.

sentence_level.sentence_text: So even before questions arise about permissions, account access or automation, the action at the center of the screenshots runs into a basic product limitation: the computer version of WeChat is not built to send red packets the way the mobile app does.
atoms: The action in the screenshots conflicts with a basic product limitation.
The computer version of WeChat is not built to send red packets like the mobile app.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The platform also said WeChat accounts and payment functions are protected by strict security measures, and that the company had not found or received reports of AI taking over accounts to send red packets as of the time the notice was issued.
atoms: The platform stated WeChat accounts and payment functions are protected by strict security measures.
The company had not found reports of AI taking over accounts to send red packets as of the notice date.
The company had not received reports of AI taking over accounts to send red packets as of the notice date.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Chinese media reports published around March 10 and 11 said WeChat personnel had also publicly dismissed the suggestion that OpenClaw or QClaw could simply be told to send money through the app.
atoms: Chinese media reports were published around March 10 and 11.
The reports stated WeChat personnel publicly dismissed the suggestion.
The suggestion was that OpenClaw or QClaw could be told to send money through the app.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Those reports drew a line between broad security concerns linked to software permissions or poor configuration and the far more specific claim circulating online about automatic WeChat red-packet transfers.
atoms: The reports distinguished between broad security concerns and specific claims about automatic red-packet transfers.
Broad security concerns are linked to software permissions or poor configuration.

sentence_level.sentence_text: For people outside China, WeChat red packets are a built-in feature that allows users to send small amounts of money in chats, often during holidays or in group conversations.
atoms: WeChat red packets are a built-in feature for sending small amounts of money.
Red packets are often used during holidays or in group conversations.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Because the tool is tied to real-name accounts and payment systems, claims that a third-party assistant could trigger it automatically can spread fast and sound believable, especially to people who do not know the app’s technical limits.
atoms: WeChat red packets are tied to real-name accounts and payment systems.
Claims about third-party assistants triggering red packets automatically can spread fast.
Such claims sound believable to people unaware of the app’s technical limits.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That appears to have helped the screenshots gain traction, even though their creator later described the example behind them as a joke.
atoms: The believability of the claim helped the screenshots gain traction.
The creator of the screenshots later described the example as a joke.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The March 12 notice also reflects a wider pattern in Chinese anti-rumor messaging as consumer AI products spread quickly.
atoms: The March 12 notice reflects a wider pattern in Chinese anti-rumor messaging.
This pattern occurs as consumer AI products spread quickly.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Authorities and company-linked accounts have warned that some tools may involve ordinary cybersecurity risks, including broad permissions, malicious plug-ins or unsafe configurations.
atoms: Authorities and company-linked accounts warned about ordinary cybersecurity risks in some tools.
Risks include broad permissions, malicious plug-ins, or unsafe configurations.

sentence_level.sentence_text: At the same time, they have argued that those risks should not be confused with dramatic stories claiming that a chatbot or assistant can instantly seize control of a payment account.
atoms: Authorities argued that ordinary cybersecurity risks should not be confused with dramatic stories.
The dramatic stories claim a chatbot or assistant can instantly seize control of a payment account.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In this case, the rumor-refuting platform said the viral story was packaged as a security warning, but did not describe a real event.
atoms: The rumor-refuting platform said the viral story was packaged as a security warning.
The platform said the story did not describe a real event.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The practical advice in the debunking was familiar.
atoms: The practical advice in the debunking was described as familiar.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Users were told to manage app permissions carefully, protect personal information, avoid clicking unknown links, and never share passwords or verification codes.
atoms: Users were advised to manage app permissions carefully.
Users were advised to protect personal information.
Users were advised to avoid clicking unknown links.
Users were advised never to share passwords or verification codes.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The notice said those steps still matter whether or not a sensational online claim later proves to be fabricated, especially when screenshots and fragments of chat are used to make an invented scenario look like a documented case.
atoms: The notice stated security steps matter even if online claims are fabricated.
The notice highlighted the risk when screenshots are used to make invented scenarios look documented.

paragraph_level: Chinese authorities and the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform officially denied on March 12, 2026, that AI tools OpenClaw or QClaw could automatically send WeChat red packets.
The viral claim originated from screenshots of group chats alleging that users could be charged (e.g., 600 yuan) without manual action via AI instructions.
The original poster of the screenshots later clarified that the images were a joke or meme, not evidence of a real financial loss.
Technically, the claim is invalid because QClaw deploys on computers, and the WeChat desktop version does not support sending red packets.
WeChat personnel and Chinese media reports from March 10-11 also dismissed the possibility of AI automatically sending money through the app.
The incident illustrates a broader pattern where ordinary cybersecurity risks (permissions, plugins) are confused with dramatic, fabricated stories about AI seizing payment control.
Authorities advised users to maintain standard security practices, such as managing permissions and protecting verification codes, regardless of the rumor's validity.
retrieval_queries: WeChat red packets automatic sending AI
WeChat desktop version send red packets
WeChat PC version red packet limitations
Cybersecurity risks broad permissions malicious plug-ins
WeChat user advice manage app permissions
WeChat mobile vs desktop red packet function

The stamp reads '谣言' (Rumor) in large characters and identifies the 'China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform'. The underlying text discusses 'OpenClaw' and 'QClaw' and mentions a loss of '600 yuan'.

retrieved_evidence.text: In recent reports on online safety and digital misinformation in China, the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform has addressed claims involving OpenClaw, QClaw and WeChat. A notice dated March 12, 2026 stated that allegations of users losing 600 yuan because these tools supposedly logged into WeChat and automatically sent red packets were unfounded. According to the platform and subsequent clarifications by the original poster, the widely circulated joking posts did not describe real financial losses, and the technical workflow described in those accounts does not match how WeChat actually operates.

In recent days, Chinese social media platforms have seen wide discussion of online posts claiming that tools referred to as OpenClaw or QClaw could be connected with the messaging app WeChat to automatically send cash gifts, or so‑called red packets, in group chats. According to people familiar with internet management work, these posts suggested that users might lose control of their accounts and funds through such a setup. Relevant departments have since paid attention to the spread of the claim and initiated checks on the technical descriptions involved, as concerns over account security continued to draw public attention.

National and local online information governance bodies have responded to the recent online discussion about alleged automated red packet sending via auxiliary tools, according to an official notice issued this week. Chinese rumor‑debunking authorities stated that, based on their verification and consultation with technical experts, the circulating claim did not match actual usage conditions on the platforms involved. The notice said relevant departments would continue to monitor the spread of such content and reminded users to rely on information released through official channels when assessing similar reports involving account and payment security.

The China Internet Joint Rumor‑Refuting Platform on March 12, 2026, released a notice addressing online claims about automated red packet sending through external tools, according to information available on its official channels. The document cited verification results provided by Chengdu Cyberspace rumor‑refuting sources and the service known as Rumor Filter, and summarized their views on the technical and factual aspects of the circulating materials. The platform said it would maintain coordination with local departments to publish similar clarifications in a timely manner when comparable content gains rapid traction on the internet.

【“用OpenClaw登录微信被刷走600块”不实】近日，有网民晒出群聊天记录截图，声称可通过向“OpenClaw / QClaw”发送指令，实现自动发送微信红包，引发关注。 经核实，该内容实为网民“玩梗”创作，最初发布相关截图的原作者也已澄清，表示并非真实事件。 从技术角度看，截图中所描述的操作不具备可行性——QClaw目前仅支持在电脑端部署，而电脑版微信并不支持发送红包。 微信账号和支付均有严格的安全保护机制。截至目前，微信官方从未发现、也未曾收到任何这类被AI“控制发红包”的反馈。提醒广大网民提高安全意识，谨慎管理应用权限，保护好个人信息，不点击不明链接，切勿泄露密码或验证码。（来源：“成都网络辟谣”微信公众号、谣言过滤器）

Following the circulation of posts alleging that external tools could control WeChat accounts to send red packets, platform representatives stated in an official communication that, as of the time relevant notices were issued, the company had not discovered such situations through its own monitoring nor received credible user reports of accounts being taken over by AI to initiate red packet transfers. According to the statement, WeChat would continue to track related discussions, collect feedback through customer service channels and cooperate with regulators to address user concerns about account and payment safety.

Financial and cybersecurity specialists consulted by authorities in recent days have underlined that WeChat accounts and associated payment services are subject to strict security protections. According to these experts, the system uses multiple layers of technical measures and management procedures aimed at preventing unauthorized access and abnormal transactions. They indicated that risk control mechanisms are continuously updated in light of new threat patterns. Officials advised users to enable recommended security options and to remain cautious about linking their accounts to unverified tools or services promoted through online posts.

Technical explanations in the March 12, 2026 clarification notice focused on the way WeChat functions on computers. According to the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform and Tencent’s published documentation, the WeChat desktop client is primarily designed for messaging, file transfer and certain work-related integrations, and does not support initiating red packets from the desktop environment. Because OpenClaw and QClaw are software tools that run on computers, experts noted that the scenario in which they directly trigger automatic red-packet sending through a desktop login is not technically feasible under WeChat’s current feature set.

Guidance from Chinese cybersecurity authorities and the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform uses the case of the OpenClaw and QClaw WeChat claims to stress broader digital hygiene. According to officials and consumer protection experts, users are advised to remain cautious about sensational posts related to WeChat payments, regularly review app permissions, keep operating systems and clients up to date, and avoid clicking suspicious links or granting remote control. They also reiterate that passwords, SMS verification codes, and payment credentials should never be shared, as social engineering remains a far more common threat than the kind of automated red-packet loss described in the disputed accounts.

Responding to questions about whether third‑party tools could trigger automated red packet functions in WeChat, technical staff familiar with the application’s product design stated this week that the desktop version of WeChat does not provide a feature for sending red packets. According to these personnel, users accessing the service on computers currently have access to a range of communication and file transfer tools, but the specific function involving cash gifts remains limited to other versions. They added that any changes to this arrangement would be subject to official product announcements and relevant regulatory requirements.

The individual identified as the original creator of the online screenshots that sparked discussion over automated red packet sending has publicly explained the background of the material, according to information released in recent days by local cyberspace authorities. The person stated that the chat images were fabricated for entertainment purposes and did not reflect a real incident. The clarification, shared through relevant platforms, emphasized that no actual group chat had experienced the described situation. Officials noted that the statement has been circulated to reduce misunderstanding among users who may have taken the earlier content as a record of real events.

Accounts from users and developers familiar with WeChat emphasize that the red-packet feature is a standard mobile payment function rather than an automated process tied to third‑party tools like OpenClaw or QClaw. On supported platforms such as the WeChat mobile app, users must actively select the red‑packet option, enter an amount, and confirm payment, often with biometric checks or a password. The verification report highlighted that this deliberate interaction design contradicts the online stories describing a desktop‑based AI control workflow in which 600 yuan could be transferred without explicit user confirmation.

According to information from local cyberspace authorities this week, the widely shared content about automated sending of red packets was mainly composed of screenshots purporting to show group chat conversations. These images, which circulated rapidly across multiple platforms, appeared to display chat members discussing red packets being issued without manual operation. Officials noted that the screenshots were forwarded in various forms, often without clear source attribution, and said that the way they were presented contributed to heightened concern among some users about potential security risks in online communication tools.

In the wake of recent public concern over alleged automated red packet sending, people familiar with the development of related tools have introduced the current application scope of QClaw. According to these sources, QClaw presently supports deployment and use on computers, with its functions operating within standard desktop environments. They noted that its existing design is oriented toward tasks compatible with conventional computer platforms, and said that any extension of its capabilities to other device types would need to undergo additional technical evaluation and compliance review by relevant parties.

Technical personnel consulted by relevant departments have assessed the online description of connecting auxiliary tools with WeChat to automatically send red packets and concluded that the specific setup shown in the screenshots does not correspond to a technically feasible process. According to these experts, several key operational links portrayed in the images lack the necessary functional support in current software and system environments. The assessment, released in a recent briefing, stressed that the scenario should be viewed as a hypothetical depiction rather than a realistically achievable method of controlling user accounts in the manner suggested.

The company had not received reports of AI taking over accounts to send red packets as of the notice date.
Chinese media reports were published around March 10 and 11.
The reports stated WeChat personnel publicly dismissed the suggestion.
The suggestion was that OpenClaw or QClaw could be told to send money through the app.
The reports distinguished between broad security concerns and specific claims about automatic red-packet transfers.
Broad security concerns are linked to software permissions or poor configuration.
WeChat red packets are a built-in feature for sending small amounts of money.
Red packets are often used during holidays or in group conversations.
WeChat red packets are tied to real-name accounts and payment systems.
Claims about third-party assistants triggering red packets automatically can spread fast.
Such claims sound believable to people unaware of the app’s technical limits.
The believability of the claim helped the screenshots gain traction.
The creator of the screenshots later described the example as a joke.
The March 12 notice reflects a wider pattern in Chinese anti-rumor messaging.
This pattern occurs as consumer AI products spread quickly.
Authorities and company-linked accounts warned about ordinary cybersecurity risks in some tools.
Risks include broad permissions, malicious plug-ins, or unsafe configurations.
Authorities argued that ordinary cybersecurity risks should not be confused with dramatic stories.
The dramatic stories claim a chatbot or assistant can instantly seize control of a payment account.
The rumor-refuting platform said the viral story was packaged as a security warning.
The platform said the story did not describe a real event.
The practical advice in the debunking was described as familiar.
Users were advised to manage app permissions carefully.
Users were advised to protect personal information.
Users were advised to avoid clicking unknown links.
Users were advised never to share passwords or verification codes.
The notice stated security steps matter even if online claims are fabricated.
The notice highlighted the risk when screenshots are used to make invented scenarios look documented.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the specific date, entities, technical limitations, and debunking narrative found in the evidence without adding fabricated details or distorting the scope.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The text is a faithful, high-precision summary of the retrieved evidence, correctly attributing the debunking to the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform and accurately reflecting the technical and factual clarifications provided.

images.explanation: The image is a screenshot of a debunked post, featuring a prominent editorial overlay stamp added by the rumor-refuting platform.

image_analysis.global_notes: The post uses a mix of edited screenshots (with overlays) and a clean stock photo. The overlays in images 1, 2, and 3 are editorial additions (stamps, highlights) rather than evidence of the rumor itself, but they constitute visual editing.

judge.level3_rationale: The post accurately reports the official debunking of the WeChat AI rumor, with all factual claims supported by evidence, though the images contain minor illustrative edits like highlight boxes and stamps.
judge.reasoning_trace: The text is fully aligned with evidence (SUPPORTED). The images contain V2 (highlights/stamps) but no V1 or C-labels. 1 L2 label (V2) -> Mostly True.
judge.core_claim: Chinese authorities and the China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform officially debunked a viral claim on March 12, 2026, stating that AI tools OpenClaw or QClaw cannot automatically send WeChat red packets, citing technical impossibilities and identifying the source screenshots as a joke.
key_subclaims: The China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform issued a denial on March 12, 2026.
The viral claim alleged that OpenClaw or QClaw could automatically send WeChat red packets, causing users to lose money (e.g., 600 yuan).
The original poster of the screenshots clarified they were a joke/meme, not a real incident.
Technically, the claim is invalid because QClaw deploys on computers, and the WeChat desktop version does not support sending red packets.
WeChat personnel and Chinese media reports from March 10-11 also dismissed the claim.
The post includes images illustrating the rumor, the debunking stamp, and the WeChat interface.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The China Internet Joint Rumor-Refuting Platform issued a denial on March 12, 2026.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the date and the issuing body.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The viral claim alleged that OpenClaw or QClaw could automatically send WeChat red packets, causing users to lose money (e.g., 600 yuan).
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the specific content of the rumor and the 600 yuan figure.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The original poster of the screenshots clarified they were a joke/meme, not a real incident.
Technically, the claim is invalid because QClaw deploys on computers, and the WeChat desktop version does not support sending red packets.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence explicitly states these technical limitations.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: WeChat personnel and Chinese media reports from March 10-11 also dismissed the claim.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms WeChat's official stance and timeline.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The post includes images illustrating the rumor, the debunking stamp, and the WeChat interface.
subclaim_findings.note: Images match the description of the rumor and the debunking notice.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post's core claim and all supporting details are fully supported by the retrieved evidence. The text accurately reports the debunking of the rumor, the technical reasons, and the source clarification. The only distortion is the presence of visual editing (V2) in the form of highlight boxes and a stamp overlay on the images, which are illustrative and do not mislead the audience or contradict the text. Since there is exactly one mild L2 label (V2) and no fabrication or contradiction, the verdict is Mostly True.
